Currently text-only, Incognito Chat will eventually support image and voice processing. WhatsApp head Will Cathcart framed the technical challenge as running "a giant phone for AI" inside a data center—maintaining smartphone-like security properties at cloud scale. A complementary feature, Side Chat, allows users to ask Meta AI about ongoing group conversations without exposing that context to other systems.

Meta invited third-party audits and will subject Incognito Chat to expert oversight. Johns Hopkins cryptographer Matt Green, who reviewed Private Processing's design, expressed confidence the system achieves its privacy objective. He emphasized, however, that secure cloud solutions create "a prominent and incredibly valuable target for attackers," introducing concentra­ted security risk that financial services and other secret-dependent systems know well.

## The Enterprise Privacy Signal

For enterprise AI adoption, Incognito Chat represents a concrete demonstration that on-device processing and server-side privacy are not binary choices. The architecture allows companies to leverage larger computational models—inherently impossible on-device—while maintaining cryptographic separation from the provider. This has implications for regulated industries where data minimization requirements conflict with AI capability needs.

The announcement carries strategic weight given Meta's competing privacy track record. This week, Meta eliminated optional end-to-end encryption from Instagram Direct Messages after years of promising default E2E coverage. That reversal suggests organizational priorities shift despite technical accomplishments. For organizations evaluating AI partnerships, Incognito Chat demonstrates viable privacy-preserving infrastructure, but Meta's broader trajectory warrants monitoring alongside technical assurances.
items.original_title: WhatsApp Adds Meta AI Chats That Are Built to Be Fully Private

items.headline: Poppy Combines Calendar, Email, Messages Into Proactive AI Dashboard
items.body:
Poppy, a new mobile app built by former Humane engineer Sai Kambampati, consolidates calendar, email, messages, and location data into a single interface, using AI to surface what matters to users at any given moment. The San Francisco startup raised $1.25 million in pre-seed funding led by Kindred Ventures, with backing from DeepMind's Logan Kilpatrick, signaling investor interest in the ambient computing problem space.

## What Poppy Does

The app connects to services including Apple Calendar, Google Calendar, Gmail, Outlook, iCloud Mail, Apple Health, Reminders, iMessage, WhatsApp, Uber, and Instacart. Users grant Poppy access to their calendar, email, messages, and location data. The system then generates proactive suggestions based on context—for example, recommending a park walk if it detects a 30-minute gap in your schedule near green space, or suggesting restaurants that match dining companions' stated food preferences. Users can also message Poppy with requests, such as flight tracking or medication reminders.

At launch, the four-person team is handling data storage with encryption and a zero-retention policy for cloud-based LLM processing. Kambampati has flagged a known limitation: iMessage access requires a Mac app due to Apple's restrictions on third-party message access, which could become problematic if the company restricts this further.

## Privacy Trade-offs and On-Device Future

Poppy's reliance on cloud AI services for inference creates a privacy surface that enterprise users and privacy-conscious consumers will scrutinize. Kambampati has indicated plans to migrate toward on-device processing within two to three years as local AI models become smaller and more capable, eliminating the need to send data to servers.

For enterprise deployments or consumer adoption at scale, the gap between current cloud dependency and the stated on-device vision matters. Organizations considering integration of such tools into workflows need clarity on data handling during the interim period. The technical direction—shifting computation to devices—aligns with broader industry movement, but the timeline remains speculative. How aggressively Poppy pursues this pivot will determine whether the product can address heightened privacy expectations, particularly as regulatory frameworks around personal data tighten.
items.original_title: Poppy debuts a proactive AI assistant to help organize your digital life
